source.title: Evaluating Medical Decision-Making Capacity in Practice

signals: Several formal assessment tools are available
capacity evaluation should be considered if there is reason to question
physicians intuitively assess capacity at every medical encounter

extraction.summary: This guideline addresses the evaluation of medical decision-making capacity - the ability of patients to understand benefits, risks, and alternatives of proposed medical treatments. The primary diagnostic approach involves assessing four key elements: understanding, appreciation, reasoning, and communication through structured clinical interviews and formal assessment tools.

population.age_criteria: Adults of all ages; specific risk factors include age <18 years and age >85 years
population.risk_criteria: Acute mental status changes, refusal of clearly beneficial treatment, chronic neurologic/psychiatric conditions, low education level, cultural/language barriers, acknowledged fear of healthcare settings
population.disease_definition: Medical decision-making capacity is the ability to understand benefits/risks/alternatives of proposed treatment, appreciate consequences, demonstrate reasoning, and communicate choice
population.exclusions: Communication barriers (hearing, vision, language, medical jargon) must be addressed before formal capacity evaluation
population.severity_staging: Capacity can vary by circumstance - patients may have capacity for simple decisions but lack capacity for complex/high-risk procedures

key_thresholds.parameter: ACE positive evaluation likelihood ratio for incapacity
Mini-Mental State Examination score for likely incapacity
key_thresholds.context: 6.3 times more likely to have incapacity

key_thresholds.parameter: Mini-Mental State Examination score unlikely incapacity
Montreal Cognitive Assessment score for incapacity
key_thresholds.context: 94% sensitivity, 90% specificity in Parkinson disease patients
Cognitive assessment tool mentioned for evaluating capacity

recommendations.recommendation: Use structured approach when assessing decision-making capacity including assessment of communication barriers, reversible causes, directed interview, and formal tools if needed
recommendations.notes: Should include the four elements: understanding, appreciation, reasoning, communication

recommendations.recommendation: Use formal assessment tool such as Aid to Capacity Evaluation (ACE) when directed interview is inconclusive
Use standard cognitive assessment instrument when patients score at extremes of scale
recommendations.notes: Very high scores favor capacity, very low scores favor incapacity

recommendations.recommendation: Consider psychiatric consultation for patients with history of schizophrenia or psychiatric/delusional disorders
recommendations.notes: Final determination remains with treating physician

recommendations.recommendation: When uncertain, err on side of determining patient has capacity
recommendations.notes: Patients presumed capable; high burden of proof needed to restrict autonomy

recommendations.recommendation: Use formal assessment tools like the ACE (Aid to Capacity Evaluation) when informal assessment suggests lack of capacity
recommendations.notes: Particularly important when patient understanding or appreciation is questionable

recommendations.recommendation: Document exact patient responses and brief evaluation in medical record
recommendations.notes: Essential for legal and clinical documentation

recommendations.recommendation: Obtain psychiatric consultation for patients with psychiatric illness affecting decision-making
recommendations.notes: Especially for patients with disorganized thinking or hallucinations

recommendations.recommendation: Identify surrogate decision makers when patient lacks capacity
recommendations.notes: Priority order: spouse, adult children, parents, siblings, other relatives

red_flags: Refusal of clearly beneficial recommended treatment without adequate reasoning
Readily agreeing to invasive/risky procedure without considering risks and benefits
Decision affected by active delusions or psychosis
Decision affected by severe depression with hopelessness/worthlessness
Inability to perform activities of daily living
Patient refusing treatment due to paranoid delusions
Emergency situations where delaying care increases risk of death or serious harm
extraction.follow_up: Repeat capacity assessment once reversible causes (infection, medication effects, metabolic derangements, delirium) have been addressed. Ongoing assessment as clinical situation changes.
extraction.escalation_path: If patient lacks capacity: identify surrogate decision maker (advance directive, medical power of attorney, closest relative in state-determined order). If no surrogate available and delay would increase risk of death/serious harm, provide emergency care without formal consent. Consider court-appointed health fiduciary if no known relatives. Psychiatric consultation recommended for complex cases involving psychiatric illness.
